# Telegram fetcher PoC plan implementation audit

Audited plan: `.github/notes/Telegram fetcher, PoC implementation plan.md`.
Scope: spec-compliance only — places where the implementation under `main.go` +
`internal/**` diverges from a contract the plan fixes. Pure code-quality issues
(hidden bugs, refactors, best practices) live in the `reviews/` siblings.

Already filed, deliberately not repeated here:

- `reports/20260517T101916Z.md` — `INDEXIT_LOG_LEVEL` unimplemented; `-v=debug`
  instead of `-v=info`; no transport backoff; summary missing `flood_waits`;
  GoReleaser `tool` identity; partial test strategy.
- `reports/20260517T101938Z.md` — `raw_hash` missing; `connecting` has no DC;
  FLOOD_WAIT wording; split summary lines; `-vv` no TL names; package layout vs
  §11; smoke-only golden tests.

Build state: `go test ./...` green; `go vet ./...` flags the known mutex copy
(tracked as a code-review item in `reviews/`, not a spec gap).

Severity: **Crit** / **High** / **Med** / **Low** / **Nit**.

## 1. PEER_FLOOD is returned without guidance

Plan §8 specifies RateGuard as the *central handler* for
`PEER_FLOOD (fail fast with guidance)`. `internal/telegram/rate.go:32-34`:

```go
if tgerr.Is(err, "PEER_FLOOD") {
    return err
}
```

The "fail fast" half is right (no retry, no sleep — `rate_test.go:78-83` pins
that), but there is **no guidance**: the user sees the raw
`rpc error code 420: PEER_FLOOD` with no hint about what it means or what to do
(the account is sending requests too aggressively; back off, reduce `--limit`,
retry later). FLOOD_WAIT gets a human line (`rate.go:61`) and cold-cache gets
`ColdPeerHint`; `PEER_FLOOD` is the one error class the plan calls out for
guidance and the one that ships without it.

**Fix.** Wrap with an actionable sentence the way `ColdPeerHint` does, e.g.
`fmt.Errorf("PEER_FLOOD: Telegram is rate-limiting this account; reduce request volume or wait before retrying: %w", err)`.

## 2. Always-on liveness markers are emitted at info level

Plan §10.1 fixes a two-tier model: at the **default level (`warn`)** a fixed set
of lifecycle markers is *always on* (`connecting…`, `authorized…`, `proxy:…`,
`FLOOD_WAIT…`, `cache: loaded/persisted`, the summary, and the heartbeat —
"this guarantees the user sees *something* … even at the default verbosity");
**per-page progress** (`dialogs: page …`) appears only at **`-v` (`info`)**.

In the implementation there is no `warn`-vs-`info` separation — *both* layers
are logged at `Info`:

- lifecycle markers: `internal/telegram/api.go:87,102,111` (`connecting` /
  `still connecting` / `connected`), `auth.go:21` (`authorized`),
  `command/telegram/common.go:67` + `auth.go:75` (`proxy`), `rate.go:61`
  (`flood wait`), `api.go:148` (heartbeat), and the `cache: loaded` /
  `cache: persisted` / `done` lines in
  `command/telegram/fetch.go:94,110,112,158,183,185`;
- per-page progress: `internal/telegram/fetch.go:97,110,216,221` — also `Info`.

This only *appears* to work because the level mapping is itself off-spec:
`internal/log/log.go:44-51` sets the **default level to `info`** (already filed
in `reports/20260517T101916Z.md` §2). The two off-spec choices cancel out. The
consequence not yet recorded: **correcting the default level to `warn` (as
§3/§10.1 require) would silently suppress every mandated always-on marker**,
because `Info < Warn`. §10.1 calls removing a previously-printed lifecycle
marker "a UX regression" — yet the straightforward fix to the level mapping
causes exactly that.

**Fix.** Give the always-on markers a level that survives the default (emit at
`Warn`, or add a dedicated "lifecycle" path that ignores the verbosity gate) and
keep per-page progress at `Info`. Then the default can be set to `warn` per
spec without losing liveness.

## 3. The timeout flag diverges in type and placement

Plan §3 lists `--timeout` as a **`fetch` common flag** of type **`duration`**
(default `0`). The implementation declares it on the **`telegram` command** as a
**`string`** (`internal/command/telegram/root.go:10,25`):

```go
timeout string
...
command.PersistentFlags().StringVar(&opt.timeout, "timeout", "0", "wall-clock timeout for Telegram operations")
```

then parses it by hand (`common.go:32`, `time.ParseDuration(opt.timeout)`).
Consequences:

1. `--help` shows `string` with default `"0"` instead of a duration flag.
2. Because it is persistent on `telegram`, it is also exposed on
   `auth login/status/logout` — outside the §3 scope, which scopes it to
   `fetch`.
3. It is absent from the README root-flags table (`README.md:81-85`).

(The bad-value handling — `--timeout=30` returns a plain error and exits `1`
instead of the usage code `2` — is a code-review item, filed in
`reviews/20260601T055834Z.md`.)

**Fix.** Use `DurationVar` and either move it under `fetch` or document the
broadened scope.

## 4. The authorized and proxy markers use non-spec wording

`reports/20260517T101938Z.md` §2-3 filed the `connecting` and `FLOOD_WAIT`
wording mismatches. Two more markers from the §10.1 example set differ:

| §10.1 contract                       | Implementation                                                |
| ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `authorized as @<username> (id=<n>)` | `authorized user_id=<n> username=<u> name=<…>` (`auth.go:21`) |
| `proxy: <type> <host>:<port>`        | `proxy type=<t> host=<h> port=<p>` (`common.go:67`)           |

Wording "may evolve" per §10.1, so this is Low — but it is part of the fixed
example set, and the README (`README.md:75-76`) further entrenches the divergent
forms.

## 5. debug uid does not echo the canonical uid

Milestone 1's demoable is `indexit telegram debug uid <s>` *echoes canonical*.
`internal/command/telegram/debug.go:34-36` JSON-encodes the parsed `PeerRef`
with no field tags, so it emits Go field names and internal bookkeeping
(`{"Kind":"channel","ID":123,"Username":"","TopicID":45,"HasTopic":true,"AnchorID":0,"HasAnchor":false}`)
instead of the canonical string the type already produces — `PeerRef.String()`
(`uid.go:73`) yields `channel:123:45`. The canonical round-trip the milestone
promises is one call away but unused.

**Fix.** Emit `ref.String()` (optionally alongside the struct under a verbose
flag), or add `json:"…"` tags so the echo is at least snake-cased and
schema-consistent with the rest of the output.

## 6. The JSONL schema and compatibility contract are undocumented

Plan §6.2 fixes the compatibility contract *as documentation*: "Versioned
`_schema_version` is not included in PoC; the discriminator + field-additive
policy is documented as the compatibility contract." Milestone 9's demoable is
"Output format documented." The README documents *that* output is JSONL on
stdout (`README.md:105-133`) but never lists the `_kind` discriminator, the
dialog/message field sets (§6.1/§6.2), or the "fields may be added, never
repurposed" promise. The stated downstream consumer — a Sparkle indexer (§1
success criteria) — has no written schema to code against and no statement of
the stability guarantee.

**Fix.** Add a short "Output schema" section enumerating the two record kinds
and the additive policy (pairs naturally with the golden-byte test gap filed in
`reports/20260517T101938Z.md` §7).

## 7. viper is linked into the binary

Plan §5.1: "The implementation lives in a small `internal/config` package
(godotenv-style parser, no viper dependency)." The package itself is viper-free
(`internal/config/parser.go`), but `viper` and its transitive encoders are still
compiled into the binary:

```
$ go list -deps ./... | grep -i viper
github.com/spf13/viper
github.com/spf13/viper/internal/encoding/{hcl,json,toml,yaml}
github.com/mitchellh/mapstructure
github.com/subosito/gotenv
```

They arrive through the inherited template's `go.octolab.org/toolkit/*`
dependencies (`go.mod:11-12,49-51`). The intent of §5.1 — keep the config path
viper-free — is met at the package boundary but defeated at the link boundary,
so Milestone 0 ("repo/toolchain refresh") is not fully done. Same root cause as
the still-`tool` GoReleaser identity in `reports/20260517T101916Z.md` §5.

**Fix.** Trim the template dependencies that pull viper, or drop the "no viper
dependency" wording from the plan so the contract matches reality.
